title: Classify Quotient Relative to 1
description: Determine whether a division expression will be less than 1, about 1, or much greater than 1 without exact computation.
condition: A division expression involving fractions and/or whole numbers is given, and the task is to compare its value to 0 or 1.
response: Reason about the sizes of the dividend and divisor to decide whether the quotient is closer to 0, closer to 1, or much greater than 1.
label: Principle / Model
example: $\frac{10}{11} \div \frac{4}{5}$ is close to $1 \div 1$, so it is closer to 1.
notes: Requires conceptual understanding of how dividing by numbers greater or less than 1 affects magnitude.

title: Compute Greatest Common Factor
description: Find the greatest common factor of two whole numbers.
condition: Given two whole numbers.
response: Identify all common factors and select the greatest one.
label: Principle / Model
example: Common factors of $36$ and $90$ include $1,2,3,6,9,18$; the GCF is $18$.

title: Compute Least Common Multiple
description: Find the least common multiple of two whole numbers.
condition: Given two whole numbers.
response: List or generate multiples of each number and select the smallest common multiple.
label: Principle / Model
example: Multiples of $4$: $4,8,12,\dots$; multiples of $11$: $11,22,33,44$; LCM is $44$.

title: Compute Profit or Loss
description: Determine whether a situation results in profit or loss and compute the difference between revenue and cost.
condition: Total revenue and total cost are given in a real-world context.
response: Compute revenue − cost; if positive, state profit and the amount; if negative, state loss and the amount.
label: Principle / Model

title: Convert Remainder to Fraction
description: Express a division result with remainder as a mixed number by placing remainder over divisor.
condition: Division result with nonzero remainder.
response: Write quotient as whole number plus remainder/divisor.
label: Principle / Model

title: Divide by a Fraction Using Reciprocal
description: Divide a number by a fraction by multiplying by its reciprocal.
condition: Expression of the form $a \div \frac{b}{c}$.
response: Rewrite as $a \times \frac{c}{b}$ and multiply.
label: Rule / Plan

title: Divide by a Multiple of Ten
description: Divide a whole number by a multiple of ten by factoring out powers of ten.
condition: Division problem where divisor is 10, 20, 30, 40, etc.
response: Rewrite as division by (non-zero digit × 10) and divide stepwise, adjusting decimal place as needed.
label: Principle / Model

title: Divide Decimal Numbers
description: Divide decimal numbers by adjusting place value and performing division.
condition: Expression of the form a ÷ b where a and/or b are decimals.
response: Adjust decimals (e.g., multiply both by powers of 10) and divide to find the quotient.
label: Principle / Model

title: Divide Mixed Numbers
description: Divide mixed numbers by converting to improper fractions and applying fraction division.
condition: Division expression involving one or more mixed numbers.
response: Convert each mixed number to an improper fraction, multiply by the reciprocal, and simplify.
label: Rule / Plan

title: Effect of Dividing by a Number Greater Than 1
description: Dividing a number by a value greater than 1 results in a smaller quotient.
condition: A division expression where the divisor is greater than 1.
response: Conclude that the quotient is less than the dividend (and possibly less than 1 if the dividend is less than the divisor).
label: Principle / Model
example: $\frac{3}{4} \div 15$ is much less than 1 because a number less than 1 is divided by 15 (>1).

title: Effect of Dividing by a Number Less Than 1
description: Dividing by a number less than 1 results in a larger quotient.
condition: A division expression where the divisor is a positive number less than 1.
response: Conclude that the quotient is greater than the dividend.
label: Principle / Model

title: Estimate Quotient Using Compatible Numbers
description: Estimate a division problem by replacing numbers with nearby compatible numbers that divide easily.
condition: Division problem with numbers that are not easy to divide mentally (e.g., 375 ÷ 19).
response: Replace with compatible numbers (e.g., 400 ÷ 20) and compute the estimated quotient.
label: Principle / Model

title: Find Rectangle Width Using Area and Length
description: Given the area and length of a rectangle, compute the width by dividing area by length.
condition: Rectangle area A and length L are given; width is unknown.
response: Compute width as A ÷ L.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Based on the area model $A = L \times W$.

title: Generate Equivalent Division Expression
description: Create an equivalent division expression by multiplying or dividing both dividend and divisor by the same nonzero power of 10.
condition: Given a division expression with decimals and asked for an equivalent expression.
response: Multiply or divide both numbers by the same power of 10 to form an equivalent quotient.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Relies on the property that multiplying dividend and divisor by the same nonzero number preserves the quotient.

title: Identify Common Factor
description: Determine whether a given number is a factor of two numbers.
condition: Given numbers $a$, $b$, and candidate factor $k$.
response: Check if $k$ divides both $a$ and $b$ evenly.
label: Rule / Plan
example: $9$ is a common factor of $36$ and $90$ because $36 \div 9 = 4$ and $90 \div 9 = 10$.
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Identify Common Multiple
description: Recognize that a common multiple is a number divisible by each of the given numbers.
condition: Given two numbers and a candidate number; determine whether the candidate is divisible by both numbers.
response: Conclude the number is a common multiple if both numbers divide it evenly.
label: Principle / Model
example: To test if 42 is a common multiple of 6 and 14: $42 \div 6 = 7$ and $42 \div 14 = 3$. Since both are whole numbers, 42 is a common multiple.

title: Interpret Division as Equal Sharing
description: Model division in word problems as distributing a total equally among a given number of groups.
condition: Word problem describes splitting a total equally into n groups.
response: Write and solve division equation total ÷ n.
label: Principle / Model
example: If $3\tfrac{1}{2}$ cups are shared among 6 jars, compute $3\tfrac{1}{2} \div 6$.

title: Interpret Division as Number of Groups
description: Model division in word problems as finding how many groups of a given size fit into a total.
condition: Word problem asks how many groups of size x can be made from total y.
response: Write and solve division equation y ÷ x.
label: Principle / Model
example: If each serving is $\frac{1}{9}$ gallon and there are $4\tfrac{2}{3}$ gallons, compute $4\tfrac{2}{3} \div \frac{1}{9}$.

title: Interpret Fraction Division in Context
description: Interpret a fraction division expression as the number of groups of a given fractional size in a quantity.
condition: Word problem representing a ÷ b where b is a fraction less than 1.
response: Interpret quotient as number of groups of size b in a.
label: Principle / Model
example: $3\tfrac{5}{6} \div \frac{2}{3}$ represents how many $\frac{2}{3}$-size servings are in $3\tfrac{5}{6}$ pizzas.

title: Interpret Tape Diagram as Division Expression
description: Translate a visual tape diagram showing equal parts into a corresponding division expression.
condition: A tape diagram shows a total length and equal fractional parts or groups.
response: Write a division expression total ÷ size_of_each_part (or equivalent) that matches the diagram.
label: Principle / Model
example: A bar labeled 1 split into fourths with 3 parts highlighted corresponds to $1 \div \frac{3}{4}$.
notes: Connects visual fraction models to symbolic division.

title: Interpret Whole-Number Result in Context
description: When dividing in a real-world purchasing context, interpret the quotient as the greatest whole number not exceeding the exact value.
condition: A division result represents number of items that can be purchased and fractional items are not allowed.
response: Take the greatest whole number less than or equal to the quotient.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Equivalent to applying the floor function in context.

title: Model Division in Word Problems
description: Translate a context involving equal groups or rates into a division expression.
condition: Word problem describing total amount and size of each group.
response: Write a division expression total ÷ group size.
label: Principle / Model
example: If  $2\frac{7}{8}$ cups are available and each batch needs $\frac{3}{4}$ cup, write $2\frac{7}{8} \div \frac{3}{4}$.

title: Multiply Decimal Numbers
description: Multiply decimal numbers and place the decimal point correctly in the product.
condition: Expression of the form a × b where a and/or b are decimals.
response: Multiply as whole numbers and place the decimal based on total decimal places.
label: Principle / Model

title: Operate with Decimals
description: Accurately add, subtract, multiply, or divide decimal numbers.
condition: Given decimal numbers and an operation (+, −, ×, ÷).
response: Perform the operation using place value alignment or standard algorithms.
label: Production / Skill

title: Represent Fraction Division with Tape Diagram
description: Draw a tape diagram partitioned into equal fractional parts to model fraction division.
condition: Prompt to represent a fraction division expression visually.
response: Partition a bar into equal fractional units and show how many divisor-sized parts fit into the dividend.
label: Production / Skill
example: To model $\frac{5}{6} \div \frac{2}{3}$, divide a bar into sixths, shade 5 parts, regroup into thirds, and count how many $\frac{2}{3}$ units fit.

title: Round to Nearest Hundredth
description: Round a decimal to the nearest hundredth using the thousandths digit.
condition: Given a decimal number with at least three decimal places.
response: Check the thousandths digit and round the hundredths digit accordingly.
label: Rule / Plan
example: $6.1275$ rounded to the nearest hundredth is $6.13$.
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Round Up for Real-World Grouping
description: When dividing to determine number of groups needed, round up if there is a remainder.
condition: Division used to find number of containers/groups needed and remainder exists.
response: Increase quotient to next whole number.
label: Concept
notes: Response is always ‘round up’ when remainder exists in this context.

title: Scale Product Using Powers of Ten
description: Use a known whole-number product to find related decimal products by adjusting place value.
condition: A known product (e.g., 28 × 13 = 364) and a related expression where one or both factors are divided by powers of ten.
response: Adjust the known product by dividing by the corresponding power(s) of ten to account for decimal factors.
label: Principle / Model

title: Simplify Fraction or Convert to Mixed Number
description: Rewrite an improper fraction in simplest form or as a mixed number.
condition: Improper fraction or reducible fraction obtained after computation.
response: Divide numerator by denominator and/or reduce by common factors.
label: Principle / Model

title: Standard Long Division
description: Use the standard long division process to compute quotient and remainder.
condition: Multi-digit dividend divided by one- or two-digit divisor.
response: Apply divide–multiply–subtract–bring down steps to find quotient (and remainder if any).
label: Production / Skill

title: Subtract to Find Change in Quantity
description: Subtract an initial value from a final value to determine the amount of increase before dividing by a rate.
condition: Initial amount, final amount, and constant growth rate are given; asked for time.
response: Compute final minus initial to find total change.
label: Principle / Model

title: Time from Growth Rate
description: Divide total change by rate to find elapsed time.
condition: Total change in quantity and constant rate per time unit are given.
response: Divide change by rate to compute time.
label: Principle / Model

title: Use Division to Find Number of Groups or Size per Group
description: When a total amount and a per-group amount (or number of groups) are given, use division to find the unknown quantity.
condition: A word problem describes a total quantity and either the size of each group or the number of groups, and asks for the missing value.
response: Write a division expression total ÷ known_quantity to represent the situation.
label: Principle / Model
example: If 4 cups make 1 gallon, then with $8\tfrac{1}{2}$ cups the gallons are $8\tfrac{1}{2} \div 4$.
notes: Includes both measurement (how many groups?) and partitive (how much per group?) interpretations.

title: Use GCF for Equal Grouping
description: Use the greatest common factor to determine the greatest number of equal groups when distributing items evenly.
condition: A word problem describes distributing two quantities into the greatest number of equal groups with no remainder.
response: Compute the GCF of the two quantities to determine the number of groups; divide each quantity by the GCF to find items per group.
label: Principle / Model
example: With 16 whistles and 40 stickers: $\gcd(16,40)=8$. So there are 8 bags. Each bag has $16 \div 8 = 2$ whistles and $40 \div 8 = 5$ stickers.
notes: Interprets GCF as maximum number of equal groups.

title: Use LCM for Repeating Events
description: Use the least common multiple to determine when two repeating events coincide.
condition: A word problem describes two events occurring at different fixed intervals and asks when they will occur together again.
response: Compute the LCM of the two intervals to determine when both events coincide.
label: Principle / Model
example: Letters every 6 days and packages every 8 days: $6 = 2 \times 3$, $8 = 2^3$. LCM $= 2^3 \times 3 = 24$. They coincide every 24 days.
